name: Mine (feldspar)
city: Lake of the Woods County Area
short_description: Historic feldspar mine in northern Minnesota offering public rockhounding for feldspar and mica specimens. Located near the Canadian border with access to glacially-influenced terrain.
description:
The Mine (feldspar) locality in Lake of the Woods County represents a significant historical mining site in Minnesota's northern mineral district. This location has produced feldspar, quartz, and mica specimens from pegmatite deposits that formed during the late Precambrian era. The site sits at approximately 1,111 feet elevation in a region shaped by Pleistocene glaciation, where glacial activity exposed mineral-rich bedrock.

Feldspar is the primary mineral of interest here, occurring as large crystals in pegmatitic intrusions common throughout this part of Minnesota. The feldspars are typically light-colored varieties including orthoclase and plagioclase, often associated with quartz and mica in this geological setting. The pegmatite mineralogy reflects the cooling of granitic magmas deep within the earth's crust.

Visitors can expect to find weathered feldspar crystals, sometimes with excellent crystal faces, embedded in quartz-rich matrix. Mica sheets and muscovite fragments are common secondary finds. The terrain is typical for this region with rocky outcrops, glacial erratics, and modest elevation changes. Late fall through early spring offers the best conditions, as accessibility improves when water levels recede.

This location requires careful research regarding current access permissions, as historical mining sites in Minnesota often have restricted access. Local rockhounding clubs can provide guidance on current collecting privileges and any seasonal closures that may apply.
history: The feldspar deposits in Lake of the Woods County formed during Precambrian pegmatite intrusion events, when granitic magmas crystallized slowly deep underground and then were exposed by Pleistocene glacial erosion. This region was mined historically for feldspar, quartz, and mica used in ceramics, glass manufacturing, and industrial applications throughout the 20th century.
tips: Search along exposed pegmatite outcrops and glacial erratics for large feldspar crystals with visible cleavage planes. Bring a hammer and chisel to carefully extract specimens from matrix without damaging the crystal faces. Look for light-colored (white to pale pink) feldspars often found adjacent to milky quartz and silvery mica sheets. The best specimens typically weather out naturally along fracture lines, so scan the ground around existing breaks in the rock.
terrain_notes: Elevation approximately 1,111 feet with rolling terrain typical of glacially-shaped northern Minnesota landscape. Rocky outcrops with exposed pegmatite bedrock, scattered glacial erratics, and understory vegetation including boreal forest species. Walking distance from parking area varies depending on access point. Terrain is moderately uneven with some loose rock and stable bedrock outcrops. Water features including small wetlands and seasonal streams are present.
written_directions: From Baudette (approximately 35 miles south), take Minnesota State Highway 11 north toward the Canadian border. Continue north approximately 20 miles toward Lake of the Woods County. The exact access road depends on current permissions and site status. Contact the Lake of the Woods County Parks Department or local rockhounding clubs for specific directions and access verification, as this historical mine site may have restricted entry.
road_conditions: County/local roads likely unpaved or partially improved. Winter conditions (October - April) may require high-clearance or all-wheel drive. Road washboard and seasonal ruts possible.
parking_notes: Limited information available. Parking likely informal at site perimeter. Verify current access and parking availability with local authorities before visiting.
collection_rules: Access to this historical mining site requires permission from current property owner or manager. Personal collection only where permitted. Respect all posted closure notices and seasonal restrictions. No motorized excavation or heavy equipment allowed. Collect only loose material and specimens that naturally weather out; do not damage living rock exposures.
rules:
Obtain permission before accessing site
No commercial collection
No motorized equipment
Respect seasonal closures
Collect loose specimens only
Do not damage active rock faces
nearest_services: Baudette, MN approximately 35 miles south, offers gas, food, lodging, and supplies. Rainy River area nearby with additional services.
accessibility_notes: Moderately uneven rocky terrain with natural rock outcrops and glacial erratics. No wheelchair access. Sturdy footwear required. Terrain navigation requires modest physical ability. Remote location with limited nearby emergency services.

faq.question: What gemstones can you find at the Mine feldspar location in Lake of the Woods County?
faq.answer: The primary mineral found here is feldspar, typically light-colored orthoclase and plagioclase varieties occurring in pegmatite deposits. Secondary finds include quartz and mica (muscovite). Feldspar crystals here are often found with excellent crystal faces and can range from small to large specimens embedded in quartz matrix.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at this Minnesota feldspar mine free or do you need to pay a fee?
faq.answer: This is a private historical mining site, so access is not free and requires permission from the property owner or manager. There is no established fee structure for public rockhounding. You must contact the property holder and local rockhounding clubs to determine current access privileges before visiting.

faq.question: Do you need a permit to collect feldspar at the Mine site in Lake of the Woods County?
faq.answer: Yes, a permit or permission from the property owner is required to access this historical mining site. Minnesota does not have a statewide permit system for rockhounding, but private land access always requires landowner consent. Contact Lake of the Woods County Parks Department or local rockhounding clubs for current access information.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding for feldspar in Lake of the Woods County?
faq.answer: October through April is optimal for visiting this location. Cooler temperatures, reduced water levels, and less vegetation make rock outcrops more accessible and visible. Winter conditions may require high-clearance vehicles. Late fall is particularly favorable before heavy snow arrives.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do you need to collect feldspar specimens at this Minnesota mine?
faq.answer: Bring a rock hammer and chisel to carefully extract feldspar crystals from matrix without damage. A hand lens helps identify crystal faces and cleavage. Sturdy gloves, boots, and weather-appropriate clothing are essential. Pack water, as the site is remote and services are 35+ miles away. A GPS device or printed map is recommended.

faq.question: Is the Mine feldspar location suitable for beginner rockhounders and families?
faq.answer: Yes, this site is beginner-friendly if access is obtained. Feldspar is relatively easy to identify with its characteristic light color and visible cleavage. Children ages 8+ can participate with adult supervision. However, the remote location (35 miles from town), moderate terrain, and access restrictions make it better suited for families with some rockhounding experience and advance planning.
